sentences.text: Yes.
My name is Joshua Degani.
My date of birth is October 26, 1924, and I was born in Leipzig.
Now, before I got the name or took the name Degani, my name was Habergrutz, so was the name of my parents.
And I changed the name in Israel together with my brother and called our-- myself-- we called ourselves Degani, which is a Hebrew word with a source of [?
Degan. ?] So as from then on, we are known as Degani.
Yes.
I will.
As a matter of fact, I prepared some small resume.
To tell it all would take a lot of time, but at least I will try to give certain short description of what it was.
My parents were quite wealthy because when my father married, he entered into a business arrangement in textiles.
We had employees and a car.
A car was something very significant at that time.
And he was a merchant, or a trader, in textiles.
And I then later on became-- wanted to become more aware of the activities when I grew up, so I tried to get some documentations.

And when I visited Leipzig a few years ago, I could find in the telephone book of [? then ?] and what do you call-- or what we call here yellow pages-- [INAUDIBLE] the registration of my father, Chaim Habergrutz, as textile merchant at the place where we lived, which was [?
Humboldtstrasse ?] 27.
And I was very happy about it.
In the telephone, we were also listed at the time that shows that we lived there, and we-- my father came there from Poland, and-- not directly.
He was before active in-- in trade in [INAUDIBLE].
But later, when he came, he continued in his profession or in his trade in Leipzig.
My mother helped him at that time, and the conditions were quite favorable for Jews.
The fact is that in Leipzig lived at that time about 20,000 Jews, and apparently, the possibilities to live in a better lifestyle than over where they came from.
Most of the Jews who came from Poland-- this shows that the prosperity was something which kept them there.
There were, of course, people who lived a longer time.
The fact is that Jews lived in Europe for, what, for about 2,000 years?
And when I looked up the historical books, I see that Jews came to Leipzig, or they are mentioned in the records of Leipzig, as of the year 1352.
And in the year 1848, the Jews were given equal rights and these equal rights were, of course, trampled on the minute Hitler came to power.
What was your particular family life like?
Did you have sisters, brothers?
I had three-- one brother who survived.
I had also three wonderful sisters who were murdered together with my mother when they were deported from Leipzig to Riga.
In the continuation of my story, I will tell you how-- what-- how I came about to know about the deportation and where from.
But the first calamity occurred with the murder of my father in Dachau.
And see, life in Leipzig, just not to jump ahead too much, was very good to me, I felt, as a child who went there to school and finished.
And I was among those who could finish eight years of schooling in Leipzig because at the end of the eight year, the Kristallnacht showed us that it could not be any continuation.
The hatred and the problems which aroused for-- for Jews and were enhanced at that time were absolutely insurmountable.
But I want to dwell another few minutes on the past in Leipzig, what still is in my mind.
And when I went through some kind of historical resume by a book which was written in Leipzig, then I come across names which show me what I-- past, what I remember, what I could remember.
There were so many charitable Jews who helped people, Jews and not Jews, like Chaim [INAUDIBLE] in 1928, and there was one other wonderful person, [INAUDIBLE].
We erected a large building, at that time the largest building in the city of Leipzig and maybe even in the country.
Here, we are now used to skyscrapers, but at that time, this was a big, big event.
And the house was called [INAUDIBLE].
It still stands.
And I revisited it 1988.

And then, to later year in 1931, I remember-- I speak now what made me remember when I read about it-- an altersheim-- and then as youngster, I remember altersheim, why it was connected to very good people and known people in Leipzig, to Jews and non-Jews alike.
It was founded by one who owned a large department-- apartment-- house for merchandise.
[INAUDIBLE] And if I'm not mistaken, his name was [INAUDIBLE], and his wife was a founder of this altersheim, which is an old age home.
And then was a foundation of one of the greatest hospitals at that time.
It was modern at that time.
This was the most modern hospital, which was established by Jews-- [INAUDIBLE].
And I remember these names very well.
Before my father escaped-- he not escaped.
Before my father-- or when my father tried to escape, he-- this was one rescue station, a temple or rescue station at [INAUDIBLE] hospital because-- and I will come to it very soon.
But before that, I wanted to dwell on the year when Hitler came to power.
This was in 1933, and I remember it very, very well.
I looked through the window, and they marched at nights with their lights-- [INAUDIBLE], they called it-- and marched.
And we were quite impressed.
I was rather impressed as a child.
I did not know what this all means, what it symbolizes, what's going to be in the future.
As a matter of fact, for many years to come, I did not know.

I just became aware when I lived in Israel, and-- or rather, when I lived in the country where escaped to, to which I will also speak, that my father was murdered in Dachau that I knew here is something very, very bad going on.
My child memory is very clear about it, very clear, because I am reminded time and again in movies and books, and myself, what I saw.
In 1935, the Nuremberg Laws came, and I already could understand what it means.
It was a matter of actions against Jews, the [?
Russian ?] laws, and all the laws which were really not something normal in the entire world.
This was something abnormal which happened in Germany.
And what-- this was made law.
A law was something which was inconceivable to humanity, but conceivable to those, and they hang to the law, and they acted according to the law, and they executed according of the law.
And they stole from Jews, their money.

They put [? on them ?] [? myriad-- ?] [? myriad ?] marks as [INAUDIBLE], as when in [INAUDIBLE], when an attack was made on one of the representatives in the French-- French embassy, which gave them the source for the Kristallnacht and for even more hatred.
And I remember well your question is that this was the hate and propaganda newspaper, paper, issued by Streicher at that time.
I remember it very well.
It was almost on each corner, like you have here at postal drop box.

So there was a there, and they made it also very cheap to buy, and they made special issues of And what they wrote in the most beautiful language-- it was forbidden for Jews to read, but I nevertheless-- I was a small-- small boy.
I did not know what consequences might have-- from time to time, I took-- I bought one, and I read.
And this was such hatred, which I still could not grasp that it is going to be to work out something very terrible in the future.
But this is how a populace was instigated, instigated to hate.
And this was called And then one other thing which I remember very, very well, when the SR marched through the city.
In their song, they incorporated a line which was called, [SPEAKING GERMAN].

And again, I did not know the horrible implication of this, but this means, and when the blood of the Jews gushes out of the wound, which was inflicted-- which is inflicted from our knives, or from the knives, then we will have it much better.
Unbelievable, and it was in such style.
It was sung in a marching-- in the marching band.
You see, it was-- and everybody was listening to it.

I was listening to it, and they have many other songs which were made so popular with the people and with the [INAUDIBLE] who believed and those who did not believe but were convinced here is something well going on for the German people because they came after a very devastating period of time of high inflation.
They were-- kind of felt as rescued by Hitler.
My family-- you see, they tried to make our life as bearable as possible.
I don't think that they ever-- I did not feel that ever tied to hem and haw.
We are in immediate danger.

Later on, while the years progressed, of course this came into [? fall. ?] But at that time, speaking of 1933, 1934, 1935, it was not yet a-- a strong a factor that our-- my family-- and my family was very big.
And my sisters, who-- my brother was born two and a half years later.
Then came Miriam, and I have pictures, which I would like later to show you.

And then came Miriam, and then came Rosa, so it was obviously a feeling of well-being, of still well-being with a natural continuation of life, without knowing the great danger standing ahead, or being ahead.
This is a very good question.
The Jews-- I remember my father had meetings in our-- arranged in our house, and they already touched on something like-- like possible emigration, and this was sent to Palestine.
And-- but it was so costly.

As far as I remember, those who could show a 20,000 pounds and could show that they will not be a kind of burden to the man that would get a-- what they called at that time a certificate, Zertifikat, and could auswandern, could go.
This is as far as I-- it's a little nebulous, but this, I could grasp at the time, that there was kind of movement to move out, to move away.

The fact is that at a later point, my father, God bless his soul, tried to escape to France, but France was conquered before it could materialize, and my father, as I mentioned before, ended up in Dachau, where he died and-- was murdered-- in 1940-- oh, no, 1941.
And generally, I have in the writings I have all these days accurate, so please forgive me if I'm not very accurate now because I try to dwell on the story rather.

But his first concentration camp was Oranienburg and from there, as I read it from documents, and I read it from documents which were made at that time by the [INAUDIBLE] in Leipzig, he was transferred to Dachau, and further source was a-- a telegram or a letter from the what we call [INAUDIBLE] that-- that they told us this-- this story.
Well, at that time, it was my grandmother.
She was a widow, and I was the grandchild who carries the name of my grandfather from her side.
And it was that she had her own apartment, but at a later point, we lived together, my grandmother, my aunt, Mary, my other aunt, [?
Lana, ?] my uncle, Max, because we-- the circumstances under the Nazis forced us to live in much more crowded conditions.

The fact is that my grandmother had in Leipzig at the time my grandfather died two houses, but the economic situation had then already deteriorated because there was some element of racial problem at that time before Hitler.
And so she had to sell the houses, and they had to survive.
But at a later point, we lived all together in-- at one-- at one place while it was before in the [?
Humboldtstrasse, ?] which-- I-- the house she owned when my grandfather died.
Later, we lived in the Nordstrasse 27.
And to backtrack a little bit, what happened-- or what happened when I was a youngster, I went to school, and it was seemingly everything pretty normal.
But I remember the great names like Felix Mendelssohn.
There was an [INAUDIBLE], a monument erected from him.
When the Nazis came, and after it stood already for 44 years, it disappeared overnight because they tried and they successfully erased all the background of Jews-- of poets, of composers, and so on.
I remember Heinrich Heine.
This name might-- might be known to many people over the world.
He was a composer of a famous song, which is still-- which was still sung in Germany, saying [SPEAKING GERMAN].
And this means I still don't know why I'm so sad and what the meaning is of it.
This is-- when people go on the Reine and travel on the Reine.
This Heinrich Heine wrote a lot of beautiful poetry and the songs were created.
And since he was a Jew, they did not mention him anymore, but his song was still used.
And [INAUDIBLE] there were many poets.
Stefan Zweig, I remember.
I read Stefan Zweig-- was an outstanding, an outstanding writer, and he committed suicide when he saw what is going to come.
He could not live anymore.
And I believe this was with his wife-- his wife and he died in Nazi Germany.
And then, I remember the day I went to the Yiddish-- to the [?
Polish ?] consulate, a Polish consulate.
This was when the hate wave increased and the Jews were-- stronger-- stronger measures were taken against the Jews, and arrests were made.
And so we were told we are going to be safer at the Polish consulate.
I was told by-- by other Jews who said go there, go there.

We all went there, and when I read-- at that time, about 1,300 Jews went to the Polish consulate just to sit there and feel safe because we were told this territory where we are going to be of the Polish consulate is safe, which was never so because this was just an assumption.
And after three days, they relaxed their threats to arrest Jews, and we came home.
But vividly in my-- in my mind is when the Jews were taken out of their apartments and put into the [INAUDIBLE], which was a very small stream with a bridge over it.
In the small stream, Jews were herded into, and they're spat upon.
Those who had beards, the beards were cut off, and so they were humiliated to a terrible, terrible degree.
This was all the beginning, and this was all before the Kristallnacht.
The Kristallnacht simplifies-- they [? are ?] simply shows the day where an organized attack was committed against Jews all over Nazi Germany.
And stores were looted, and synagogues were burnt.
And I went through the streets, and I saw myself the Sefer Torah, the holy books-- all was thrown out.
And Leipzig, all the Jewish-- most of the Jewish holy institutions were in fire.
And there, they wrote about it, and there are pictures about it, and this became then history.
They saw the stones which were taken, which will remained after the-- after the fire, and that they sold it to builders.
And they burned Jewish merchandising houses, like [INAUDIBLE] and so on.
And this I remember.
You see, you asked what my memories as a child--
I saw these things happen while they were in process.
While they were in process, I saw it.
I saw with my eyes after the aftermath, after the burning of the synagogues.
And it was-- in my memory, it was a day where I went to school.
It was one of the last days of school where this happened.
[INAUDIBLE] ordered to come to school for an explanation.
The teachers there in that Jewish school-- we could not go anymore to any other school-- explained to us what it is and we should go home.
And we went home, and on the way home, we saw-- I saw the disasters.
My mother did not make because there was really nothing which could be touched.
As far as I remember, the countries did not want to absorb Jews.
We tried.
We tried.
And as a matter of fact, I also personally tried as child.
I knew already in 1938, '39, to write letters, and I wrote pretty good letters.
And I wrote to the different consulates to help us, and this especially so after my father was arrested in 1938.
He tried to escape all the misery by all ways and means.
As I mentioned before, the Jews were herded into the [INAUDIBLE], which is a small stream in Leipzig.
This was under a certain bridge used for-- Jews were humiliated.
And he tried to be-- to show that he is injured, and I remember in [INAUDIBLE] Hospital, he was staying there as a sick man.
And the Jewish doctors put some kind of bandages on his feet, but he could not-- he could not succeed.
It was felt that he was--
He was eventually arrested and sent to the concentration camp.
Yes.
All the-- all the men were in the most immediate danger, but also all of the women, as later shown.
Women and men and children, they were gradually taken and deported to various places from Riga, where my mother and three sisters were deported, to-- to Majdanek, to Theresienstadt and so on.
And there is a clear summary where everybody was deported and at what time, and I have such documentation.
I am not cognizant of-- of all the preparations which were made because my fate was something different up to a certain point.
I succeeded-- or rather, with the help of the [INAUDIBLE] I was sent to a [INAUDIBLE] place.
But this came after I had to do [INAUDIBLE] or [INAUDIBLE] or forced labor in Leipzig.
